tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

response.delta: me organize the available typenames for the user.

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

| #
ando` | A flexible/generic object type that
Represents a function with name, description, input/output schemas, services, and bin

dings. |
| 3 | `org.dxos.type.organization` | An organization with fields like name, description, status, image

, website, and location. |
| 4 | `org.dxos.type.person` | A person/contact with fields like full

name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, job title, organization, and more. |
| 5 | `org.dxos.type
.tag` | A tag with a label and optional hue (color), used to tag

other objects. |
| 6 | `org.dxos.type.trigger` | A trigger configuration that links to a function and defines
when it should fire (email, feed, subscription, timer, or webhook). |
Let me know if you'd like to explore any of these in more detail or perform any operations!

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

# DXOS — The Decentralized Operating System

## Overview
DXOS is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers.
DXOS is the Decentralized Operating System — a platform for building fully decentralized applications.

The project is setting a new standard for how modern software is built by reducing reliance on centralized servers and focusing on scalable peer-to-peer data networks. The framework empowers anyone to create real-time collaborative tools within a privacy-focused, local-first workspace that is enhanced by AI capabilities.
## Company Details
DXOS was founded in 2021 and is headquartered in New York, NY. Its primary industry is Software Development Applications, and it is a private company.
DXOS was created by a small core team of developers from many countries.
Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States) have invested in DXOS.

BlueYard Capital, a Berlin/US-based VC firm, is a notable investor that also backs other decentralized infrastructure projects like Protocol Labs and Ethereum.

## Core Technology Stack

The DXOS SDK is built around three main pillars:

### 1. ECHO (Database)
ECHO is an open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage.
ECHO serves as a peer-to-peer database and reactive state container for offline-first, real-time, collaborative apps.
### 2. HALO (Identity)
HALO seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.
HALO is a decentralized identity and access control protocol that makes it easier to build collaborative applications.
### 3. MESH (Networking)
MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.
## How It Works
DXOS leverages Automerge — a powerful CRDT that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.
The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell. Everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer.
It automatically synchronizes data across networked devices using WebRTC for cloudless peer-to-peer networking.
There are still signaling servers for peer discovery, but all data exchange happens peer-to-peer, and no servers have access to the data.
## SDK & Developer Tools
The main repository provides a TypeScript implementation of the DXOS protocols, SDK, toolchain and Composer.
DXOS wraps its three core components together in a set of SDKs (currently, React and TypeScript) that enable developers to deliver sophisticated applications quickly.
DXOS also provides developer tooling, including
debugging tools for the DXOS Client in the Chrome developer console, allowing you to connect to the DXOS Client to visualize and debug the state of the application.
## Composer — Flagship Application
DXOS is also the home of Composer — an extensible IDE, built for both real time and async collaboration.
Composer is a collaborative productivity app where developers can build and organize knowledge, extend with custom data and UI, and run private AI against their knowledge locally.

Key properties of Composer:

- **Extensibility**:
Everything inside of Composer is a plugin. This means you can leverage core functionality or build out whatever you need to take your programmatic workflows to the next level.
- **Collaboration**:
Composer has real-time multiplayer implemented through a local-first model. This makes a lot of things better: from meeting notes to working on airplanes.
- **Privacy**:
Composer is built on the peer-to-peer protocols of DXOS which are designed to be private by default.
- **Rich Content**:
Composer blends the power of popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) while allowing you to build plugins, deploy custom functions and leverage modern LLMs.
- **Data Ownership**:
You own everything inside of Composer. No spyware, no algorithms, no lock-in.
## Agents

DXOS is a decentralized and privacy-preserving platform which communicates peer-to-peer. When all the peers go offline, it's impossible for any new peers to replicate. This is where agents come in. Agents are just like any other peer, but are assumed to have more uptime on the network. They should typically be running on servers or desktop machines which are always plugged in.
DXOS is also launching a fully-managed service to host agents.

If you don't want to run your own server, the managed service is a convenient alternative. The other big reason for choosing the managed service besides great uptime, network speeds, and managed disk space, is it's a simple and convenient offsite backup for your ECHO data and HALO identity.
## Current Status

The DXOS SDK is under active development, however its current focus is building features and functionality that enables the development of Composer. To this end, they are currently only able to provide limited support to developers using the SDK.
Composer is not yet ready for production. There are bugs, and breaking changes may occur.
## Community & Open Source
The DXOS Community is made of a diverse group of open source developers from all over the world.
DXOS has 64 repositories available on GitHub.

The project actively engages developers through GitHub and Discord.

---

# DXOS — The Decentralized Operating System

## Overview

DXOS is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers. It is the Decentralized Operating System — a platform for building fully decentralized applications. The project is setting a new standard for how modern software is built by reducing reliance on centralized servers and focusing on scalable peer-to-peer data networks. The framework empowers anyone to create real-time collaborative tools within a privacy-focused, local-first workspace that is enhanced by AI capabilities.

## Company Details

DXOS was founded in 2021 and is headquartered in New York, NY. Its primary industry is Software Development Applications, and it is a private company. DXOS was created by a small core team of developers from many countries. Known investors include Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States).

## Core Technology Stack

The DXOS SDK is built around three main pillars:

### 1. ECHO (Database)
ECHO is an open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage. It serves as a peer-to-peer database and reactive state container for offline-first, real-time, collaborative apps.

### 2. HALO (Identity)
HALO seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices. It is a decentralized identity and access control protocol that makes it easier to build collaborative applications.

### 3. MESH (Networking)
MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.

## How It Works

DXOS leverages Automerge — a powerful CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type) that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict. The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell; everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer. Data is automatically synchronized across networked devices using WebRTC for cloudless peer-to-peer networking. There are still signaling servers for peer discovery, but all data exchange happens peer-to-peer, and no servers have access to the data.

## SDK & Developer Tools

The main repository provides a TypeScript implementation of the DXOS protocols, SDK, toolchain and Composer. DXOS wraps its three core components together in a set of SDKs (currently React and TypeScript) that enable developers to deliver sophisticated applications quickly. DXOS also provides developer tooling, including debugging tools for the DXOS Client in the Chrome developer console.

## Composer — Flagship Application

DXOS is also the home of Composer — an extensible IDE, built for both real time and async collaboration. Composer is a collaborative productivity app where developers can build and organize knowledge, extend with custom data and UI, and run private AI against their knowledge locally.

Key properties of Composer:
- **Extensibility**: Everything inside of Composer is a plugin, allowing leverage of core functionality or custom builds.
- **Collaboration**: Real-time multiplayer implemented through a local-first model.
- **Privacy**: Built on the peer-to-peer protocols of DXOS, designed to be private by default.
- **Rich Content**: Blends popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) with plugin support, custom functions, and modern LLM integration.
- **Data Ownership**: Users own everything inside Composer — no spyware, no algorithms, no lock-in.

## Agents

DXOS supports agents — peers with higher uptime that help replicate data when other peers are offline. Agents are typically running on servers or desktop machines. DXOS also offers a fully-managed service to host agents, providing uptime, network speeds, managed disk space, and offsite backup for ECHO data and HALO identity.

## Current Status

The DXOS SDK is under active development with a current focus on building features and functionality for Composer. Composer is not yet ready for production and breaking changes may occur.

## Community & Open Source

The DXOS Community is made of a diverse group of open source developers from all over the world, with 64 repositories available on GitHub. The project actively engages developers through GitHub and Discord.

---

**In summary**, DXOS is a pioneering open-source platform aimed at decentralizing application development by providing a complete toolkit — spanning databases (ECHO), identity (HALO), and networking (MESH) — that allows developers to build collaborative, privacy-preserving, local-first applications without relying on centralized cloud infrastructure. Its flagship product, Composer, showcases these capabilities as an extensible, plugin-driven collaborative workspace.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

- <cite index="39-3,39-4">Composer is built with extensibility at its core. Everything within the application is built using the same App Framework APIs, from the UI down to data and business logic.</cite>
